name: Chest & Triceps - Heavy Compound Focus

exercises.name: Barbell Bench Press
exercises.notes: Heaviest lift of the day — go first while fresh. Retract shoulder blades before unracking.

exercises.name: Incline Dumbbell Press
exercises.notes: 30-45 degree bench. Control the negative, don't bounce at the bottom.

exercises.name: Weighted Dips
exercises.notes: Lean forward slightly for more chest emphasis. Add weight belt once bodyweight feels easy.

exercises.name: Cable Chest Fly
exercises.notes: Squeeze at the midline for a full second.

exercises.name: Close-Grip Bench Press
exercises.notes: Hands just inside shoulder width. Keep elbows tucked to protect the joint.

exercises.name: Overhead Cable Triceps Extension
exercises.notes: Keep elbows fixed pointing forward, only the forearm moves.

cooldown: Doorway chest stretch, 30 sec each side
Overhead triceps stretch, 30 sec each side
Cross-body shoulder stretch, 30 sec each side
coachTips: Breathe out on the push, in on the lower — never hold your breath under a heavy bar.
If your bench press stalls, drop the weight 10% and rebuild rather than grinding ugly reps.
At 108kg, joint stress on the shoulders adds up fast — stop a rep short of failure on pressing movements.

name: Chest & Triceps - Upper Chest Emphasis

exercises.name: Incline Barbell Press
exercises.notes: 30 degree incline max — steeper turns this into a shoulder press.

exercises.name: Flat Dumbbell Press
exercises.notes: Full stretch at the bottom, press until arms are almost locked out.

exercises.name: Machine Chest Press
exercises.notes: Good option to push closer to failure safely since the machine spots you.

exercises.name: Low-to-High Cable Fly
exercises.notes: Targets the upper chest fibers specifically.

exercises.name: Diamond Push-Ups
exercises.notes: Elbows close to the body. Stop 1-2 reps before form breaks down.

exercises.name: Rope Triceps Pushdown
exercises.notes: Spread the rope apart at the bottom for a full contraction.

cooldown: Doorway chest stretch, 30 sec each side
Standing triceps overhead stretch, 30 sec each side
Child's pose, 45 sec
coachTips: Upper chest is stubborn for most lifters — the incline angle matters more than the weight on the bar.
If your wrists ache on diamond push-ups, do them on dumbbell handles instead of flat on the floor.
Log your rowing pace each week — a faster 500m split at the same heart rate is real conditioning progress.

name: Chest & Triceps - High Volume Pump

exercises.name: Dumbbell Bench Press
exercises.notes: Lighter than your heavy day — this session is about total volume, not max weight.

exercises.name: Incline Dumbbell Fly
exercises.notes: Slight bend in the elbow throughout, don't let it turn into a press.

exercises.name: Machine Chest Press
exercises.notes: Drop set on the final set — reduce weight 20% and continue to failure.

exercises.name: Cable Crossover
exercises.notes: Cross hands over at the bottom for a longer squeeze.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Skull Crushers
exercises.notes: Keep upper arms vertical, only the forearm swings.

exercises.name: Cable Triceps Kickback
exercises.notes: Squeeze hard at full extension for a full second.

cooldown: Doorway chest stretch, 30 sec each side
Overhead triceps stretch, 30 sec each side
coachTips: This is a pump day — chase the burn, not the weight on the dumbbells.
Rest periods are shorter here on purpose to keep the muscle under tension longer overall.
Good day to slot in after a heavier chest session earlier in the week.

name: Chest & Triceps - Bodyweight & Dumbbell Hybrid
warmup: 5 min jump rope or brisk walk

exercises.name: Standard Push-Ups
exercises.notes: Full range, chest to a fist's height off the floor.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Floor Press
exercises.notes: Great joint-friendly alternative to bench press — the floor limits range and protects the shoulder.

exercises.name: Decline Push-Ups
exercises.notes: Feet elevated on a bench, targets upper chest.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Pullover
exercises.notes: Light weight — this is about stretch and chest expansion, not load.

exercises.name: Bench Dips
exercises.notes: Keep hips close to the bench to protect the shoulder joint.

exercises.name: Close-Grip Push-Ups
exercises.notes: Finisher — hands under the chest, elbows tucked.

cooldown: Doorway chest stretch, 30 sec each side
Cross-body shoulder stretch, 30 sec each side
coachTips: This session travels well — most of it works with just dumbbells and a bench.
Push-up volume adds up fast at 108kg — that's a genuinely heavy load moving through the chest and triceps.
If AMRAP sets exceed 20 reps, add a weighted vest or slow the tempo instead of just doing more reps.

name: Chest & Triceps - Machine-Based Joint-Friendly

exercises.name: Machine Chest Press
exercises.notes: Fixed path takes stabilizer demand out of the equation — good for a lower-joint-stress heavy day.

exercises.name: Pec Deck Fly
exercises.notes: Adjust seat so handles are at chest height at the start position.

exercises.name: Incline Machine Press
exercises.notes: Upper chest focus without the stability demand of free weights.

exercises.name: Cable Fly (Standing)
exercises.notes: Slight forward lean, squeeze hands together in front of the chest.

exercises.name: Triceps Pushdown (Machine)
exercises.notes: Straight bar or rope, either works — pick whichever feels smoother on the elbow.

exercises.name: Assisted Dip Machine
exercises.notes: Use just enough assistance to hit clean reps with good depth.

cooldown: Doorway chest stretch, 30 sec each side
Overhead triceps stretch, 30 sec each side
coachTips: This is a good option on weeks where your shoulders or wrists feel beat up from barbell work.
Machines let you push closer to true failure safely — use that on the last set of each exercise.
Don't skip this variation just because it's 'easier' on paper — the stimulus is just as real.

name: Back & Biceps - Heavy Pulling Focus

exercises.name: Deadlift
exercises.notes: Reset your brace between every rep. This is your heaviest lift — no ego reps.

exercises.name: Weighted Pull-Ups
exercises.notes: Use assistance band if bodyweight x4 sets isn't achievable yet — still counts.

exercises.name: Barbell Bent-Over Row
exercises.notes: Hinge at the hips, flat back, pull to the lower ribs.

exercises.name: Seated Cable Row
exercises.notes: Pull with the elbows, not the hands — think 'shoulder blades together'.

exercises.name: Barbell Curl
exercises.notes: No swinging — if you need momentum, drop the weight.

exercises.name: Hammer Curl
exercises.notes: Neutral grip, hits the brachialis and forearms.

cooldown: Lat stretch on rack, 30 sec each side
Seated forward fold, 45 sec
Biceps wall stretch, 30 sec each side
coachTips: Deadlifts are demanding on the CNS at your bodyweight — if grip fatigues first, use straps on the last 1-2 sets only.
Row variations should feel like your shoulder blades are doing the work, not your biceps.
Log your pull-up reps every session — this is one of the clearest strength markers as bodyweight comes down.

name: Back & Biceps - Width & Thickness

exercises.name: Lat Pulldown (Wide Grip)
exercises.notes: Pull to the collarbone, lean back slightly — no swinging the torso.

exercises.name: T-Bar Row
exercises.notes: Chest supported if your gym has the pad — takes lower back load off.

exercises.name: Single-Arm Dumbbell Row
exercises.notes: Brace on the bench, drive the elbow back and up toward the hip.

exercises.name: Close-Grip Lat Pulldown
exercises.notes: V-bar handle, targets lower lat fibers.

exercises.name: Incline Dumbbell Curl
exercises.notes: Full stretch at the bottom — this is the main benefit of the incline angle.

exercises.name: Cable Curl
exercises.notes: Constant tension throughout, unlike free weights.

cooldown: Lat stretch on rack, 30 sec each side
Cat-cow, 8 reps
Biceps wall stretch, 30 sec each side
coachTips: Wide-grip work builds width, close-grip and rows build thickness — this session hits both.
Keep the assault bike interval fast but controlled — this is a conditioning finisher, not a max effort test.
Squeeze your shoulder blades together at the top of every row for a full second before releasing.

name: Back & Biceps - Dumbbell Only

exercises.name: Dumbbell Bent-Over Row
exercises.notes: Flat back throughout, hinge from the hips.

exercises.name: Single-Arm Dumbbell Row
exercises.notes: Full stretch at the bottom, drive the elbow high at the top.

exercises.name: Renegade Row
exercises.notes: Wide stance for stability, brace the core hard to resist rotation.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Pullover
exercises.notes: Slow and controlled, feel the stretch through the lats.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Curl
exercises.notes: Alternating or both arms together, either works.

exercises.name: Concentration Curl
exercises.notes: Elbow braced against the inner thigh, isolates the biceps peak.

cooldown: Lat stretch on rack, 30 sec each side
Biceps wall stretch, 30 sec each side
Child's pose, 45 sec
coachTips: This is a great travel-friendly session — dumbbells and a bench cover the whole workout.
Renegade rows are a core exercise disguised as a back exercise — keep the hips level, don't let them rock.
If your lower back fatigues before your lats do, drop the row weight and focus on the mind-muscle connection instead.

name: Back & Biceps - Pull-Up Progression

exercises.name: Pull-Ups
exercises.notes: Track total reps across all 5 sets — that running total is your progress marker.

exercises.name: Chin-Ups
exercises.notes: Underhand grip — noticeably more biceps involvement than the pull-up.

exercises.name: Lat Pulldown
exercises.notes: Use this to keep pulling volume up once bodyweight sets fatigue you.

exercises.name: Face Pull
exercises.notes: Rope at upper-face height, pull toward the forehead — great rear delt and posture work.

exercises.name: EZ-Bar Curl
exercises.notes: The angled grip is easier on the wrists than a straight bar.

exercises.name: Reverse Curl
exercises.notes: Overhand grip, builds forearm and grip strength for your pulling lifts.

cooldown: Dead hang, 30 sec
Lat stretch on rack, 30 sec each side
Forearm stretch, 20 sec each side
coachTips: At 108kg, every pull-up rep is a serious relative-strength achievement — bodyweight coming down will show up here fastest.
If AMRAP sets drop under 3 reps, switch to banded assistance for the remaining sets rather than grinding singles.
Face pulls are cheap insurance for shoulder health — don't skip them even on a tight schedule.

name: Back & Biceps - Machine Circuit

exercises.name: Seated Row Machine
exercises.notes: Chest against the pad, pull with the elbows past your ribs.

exercises.name: Lat Pulldown Machine
exercises.notes: Alternate grip weekly between wide and close for balanced development.

exercises.name: Machine Row (Single Arm)
exercises.notes: Fixes any left/right imbalance that barbell rows can hide.

exercises.name: Rear Delt Machine Fly
exercises.notes: Light weight, focus on squeezing the shoulder blades together.

exercises.name: Cable Curl (Machine Handle)
exercises.notes: Constant tension throughout the whole rep, unlike a dumbbell curl.

exercises.name: Preacher Curl Machine
exercises.notes: Pad locks the upper arm in place — pure biceps isolation.

cooldown: Lat stretch on rack, 30 sec each side
Biceps wall stretch, 30 sec each side
coachTips: This is a lower-fatigue session — good to slot in the day after a heavy deadlift day.
Machines let you isolate left/right imbalances that barbell work can mask — pay attention if one side is noticeably weaker.
Still push these sets close to failure — 'machine day' doesn't mean 'easy day'.

name: Legs - Heavy Squat Focus

exercises.name: Barbell Back Squat
exercises.notes: Depth to at least parallel. At 108kg your knees will feel this — warm up thoroughly before the first working set.

exercises.name: Romanian Deadlift
exercises.notes: Soft knee bend, hinge from the hips, feel the hamstring stretch.

exercises.name: Leg Press
exercises.notes: Don't lock the knees out hard at the top — keep slight bend to protect the joint.

exercises.name: Walking Lunges
exercises.notes: Dumbbells in hand, torso upright, front knee tracks over the toes.

exercises.name: Leg Curl (Machine)
exercises.notes: Slow negative — hamstrings are often the weak link in the chain.

exercises.name: Standing Calf Raise
exercises.notes: Full stretch at the bottom, full contraction at the top.

cooldown: Standing quad stretch, 30 sec each side
Seated hamstring stretch, 30 sec each side
Pigeon pose, 30 sec each side
coachTips: Squatting at 108kg puts real compressive load through the knees and lower back — never sacrifice depth or bar path for extra weight.
Leg day burns the most calories of any session in this library — fuel it properly beforehand.
If knees ache the next day, swap barbell back squat for leg press for a few weeks and reassess.

name: Legs - Posterior Chain Focus

exercises.name: Conventional Deadlift
exercises.notes: Reset fully between reps — this is a posterior-chain-dominant session so make it count.

exercises.name: Hip Thrust
exercises.notes: Barbell across the hips, pad underneath. Full lockout, squeeze glutes hard at the top.

exercises.name: Leg Curl (Machine)
exercises.notes: Isolated hamstring work to complement the deadlift and hip thrust.

exercises.name: Bulgarian Split Squat
exercises.notes: Rear foot elevated, most of the weight through the front heel.

exercises.name: Cable Pull-Through
exercises.notes: Great teaching tool for the hip-hinge pattern with less spinal load than a deadlift.

exercises.name: Seated Calf Raise
exercises.notes: Targets the soleus, complements the standing variation.

cooldown: Seated hamstring stretch, 30 sec each side
Figure-4 glute stretch, 30 sec each side
Child's pose, 45 sec
coachTips: Hip thrusts are one of the best glute-builders available — don't rush the lockout, hold the squeeze.
Bulgarian split squats expose left/right imbalances fast — expect one leg to be noticeably harder.
Deadlift volume is intentionally lower here since hip thrust and split squats add plenty of posterior-chain fatigue on top.

name: Legs - Quad-Dominant Machine Session

exercises.name: Leg Press
exercises.notes: A joint-friendly way to load the quads heavily without the spinal demand of a squat.

exercises.name: Hack Squat Machine
exercises.notes: Feet slightly forward on the platform for more quad emphasis.

exercises.name: Leg Extension
exercises.notes: Pause and squeeze for a full second at the top of every rep.

exercises.name: Goblet Squat
exercises.notes: Dumbbell held at the chest, great for reinforcing an upright torso.

exercises.name: Walking Lunges
exercises.notes: Finisher — keep the pace controlled even as legs fatigue.

exercises.name: Standing Calf Raise
exercises.notes: Full range of motion, don't bounce.

cooldown: Standing quad stretch, 30 sec each side
Calf stretch on wall, 30 sec each side
coachTips: Machine-based leg days are a good rotation option to keep knees fresh between heavier barbell squat sessions.
Leg press foot placement changes the emphasis — higher on the platform shifts more to glutes and hamstrings.
This session is genuinely tough despite being lower-impact — don't underestimate it on the schedule.

name: Legs - Unilateral Strength & Stability
warmup: Single-leg balance hold, 2x20 sec each side

exercises.name: Bulgarian Split Squat
exercises.notes: The main event today — expect real fatigue by the last set on each leg.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Step-Up
exercises.notes: Use a bench roughly knee height, drive through the whole foot.

exercises.name: Single-Leg Romanian Deadlift
exercises.notes: Light dumbbells, focus on balance and hip control over load.

exercises.name: Lateral Lunge
exercises.notes: Trains a movement pattern most gym programs skip entirely.

exercises.name: Goblet Squat
exercises.notes: Bilateral finisher after all the single-leg work.

exercises.name: Standing Calf Raise (Single Leg)
exercises.notes: Hold onto something for balance if needed.

cooldown: Standing quad stretch, 30 sec each side
Figure-4 glute stretch, 30 sec each side
Hip flexor stretch, 30 sec each side
coachTips: Unilateral work exposes and fixes strength imbalances that bilateral squats and presses hide.
Balance will be the limiting factor before strength on some of these — that's normal, it improves fast with practice.
This is a lighter-load, higher-control session — a good rotation choice the week after a heavy squat or deadlift day.

name: Legs - Glute & Hamstring Isolation

exercises.name: Hip Thrust (Machine or Barbell)
exercises.notes: Full lockout every rep, hold the squeeze at the top for a full second.

exercises.name: Leg Curl (Machine)
exercises.notes: Slow, controlled negative — resist the urge to just drop the weight back down.

exercises.name: Cable Kickback
exercises.notes: Ankle cuff attachment, squeeze the glute hard at the top of each rep.

exercises.name: Romanian Deadlift (Dumbbell)
exercises.notes: Moderate weight, focus on the hamstring stretch at the bottom.

exercises.name: Banded Lateral Walk
exercises.notes: Band above the knees, stay low the whole set — great glute-medius work.

exercises.name: Glute Bridge (Bodyweight)
exercises.notes: Finisher, pure burn — squeeze at the top of every rep.

cooldown: Figure-4 glute stretch, 30 sec each side
Seated hamstring stretch, 30 sec each side
coachTips: This session is lower-impact on the knees and spine than a squat/deadlift day — good for a recovery-conscious week.
Mind-muscle connection matters more here than the load on the bar — slow down and feel the target muscle work.
Banded lateral walks look easy and are not — keep tension on the band the entire set, don't let your feet drift together.

name: Shoulders - Heavy Press Focus

exercises.name: Standing Barbell Overhead Press
exercises.notes: Brace the core hard, don't let the lower back arch to compensate.

exercises.name: Seated Dumbbell Press
exercises.notes: Backrest supports the spine so you can push a little harder here than standing.

exercises.name: Cable Lateral Raise
exercises.notes: Constant tension beats dumbbells for this movement — lead with the elbow, not the hand.

exercises.name: Rear Delt Fly (Cable or Dumbbell)
exercises.notes: Most neglected head of the shoulder — don't skip it.

exercises.name: Barbell Upright Row
exercises.notes: Wide grip reduces shoulder impingement risk versus a narrow grip.

exercises.name: Front Plate Raise
exercises.notes: Light plate, controlled tempo — front delts get plenty of work from pressing already.

cooldown: Cross-body shoulder stretch, 30 sec each side
Overhead triceps stretch, 30 sec each side
Doorway chest stretch, 30 sec each side
coachTips: Overhead pressing is demanding on the lower back if you lean back to compensate — keep ribs down and glutes squeezed.
Lateral raises should never involve momentum — if you're swinging the weight up, it's too heavy.
Balance pressing volume with rear delt and upper back work every single session to protect shoulder health long-term.

name: Shoulders - 3D Delt Development

exercises.name: Machine Shoulder Press
exercises.notes: Fixed path is joint-friendly and lets you push closer to failure safely.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Lateral Raise
exercises.notes: Slight bend in the elbows, raise to shoulder height — no higher.

exercises.name: Cable Face Pull
exercises.notes: Pull to the forehead with elbows high — targets rear delts and upper back.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Front Raise
exercises.notes: Alternate arms, control the descent.

exercises.name: Reverse Pec Deck
exercises.notes: Rear delt isolation, squeeze the shoulder blades at the peak.

exercises.name: Shrugs (Dumbbell)
exercises.notes: Straight up and down, no rolling the shoulders.

cooldown: Cross-body shoulder stretch, 30 sec each side
Neck stretch, 20 sec each side
coachTips: '3D' delts means training all three heads — front, side, and rear — every session, not just pressing.
This session prioritizes the side and rear delts, which are usually the most underdeveloped for lifters who only bench and press.
Shrugs should feel like straight-line elevation — resist the urge to roll the shoulders forward or back.

name: Shoulders - Push Press & Power

exercises.name: Push Press
exercises.notes: Small dip and drive with the legs to help launch the bar — this trains power, not just strength.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Arnold Press
exercises.notes: The rotation hits the front and side delts through a longer range than a standard press.

exercises.name: Kettlebell Overhead Carry
exercises.notes: Arm locked out overhead, walk slowly and controlled — huge shoulder stability builder.

exercises.name: Cable Lateral Raise
exercises.notes: Constant tension complements the power work earlier in the session.

exercises.name: Face Pull
exercises.notes: Non-negotiable after heavy pressing — keeps the rear delts and rotator cuff healthy.

cooldown: Cross-body shoulder stretch, 30 sec each side
Overhead triceps stretch, 30 sec each side
coachTips: Push press uses leg drive on purpose — it's a different training stimulus than a strict press, not a shortcut.
Overhead carries look simple and expose weak stabilizers fast — start lighter than you think you need.
This session's higher power output means you'll want a longer rest between sets — don't rush it.

name: Shoulders - High Volume Isolation

exercises.name: Dumbbell Lateral Raise
exercises.notes: Light weight, chase the burn — this is a pump-focused session.

exercises.name: Cable Front Raise
exercises.notes: Rope attachment, alternating arms.

exercises.name: Bent-Over Dumbbell Rear Fly
exercises.notes: Hinge at the hips, flat back, small controlled arc.

exercises.name: 1.5 Rep Lateral Raise
exercises.notes: Full rep then half rep at the top counts as one — extra time under tension.

exercises.name: Cable Y-Raise
exercises.notes: Arms form a Y shape, targets the lower traps and rotator cuff.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Shrugs
exercises.notes: Finisher, straight up-and-down motion only.

cooldown: Cross-body shoulder stretch, 30 sec each side
Neck stretch, 20 sec each side
coachTips: No heavy pressing today — this session is designed to add pure volume without adding to your joint fatigue.
Good option the day after a heavy chest or shoulder press session.
Keep rest periods short on purpose — this is about accumulated time under tension, not maximal loads.

name: Shoulders - Stability & Rotator Cuff Health

exercises.name: Landmine Press
exercises.notes: Angled path is easier on the shoulder joint than a straight overhead press.

exercises.name: Band External Rotation
exercises.notes: Elbow pinned to your side, light resistance — this is rehab-style, not a strength exercise.

exercises.name: Cable Face Pull
exercises.notes: Slightly higher pull angle than usual, elbows above shoulder height.

exercises.name: Single-Arm Cable Lateral Raise
exercises.notes: Isolates one side at a time to correct imbalances.

exercises.name: Scapular Wall Slides
exercises.notes: Back against a wall, slide arms up and down keeping contact the whole time.

exercises.name: Kettlebell Overhead Carry
exercises.notes: Light weight, this is about control and stability, not load.

cooldown: Cross-body shoulder stretch, 30 sec each side
Doorway chest stretch, 30 sec each side
Neck stretch, 20 sec each side
coachTips: This is a 'prehab' session — the goal is shoulder longevity, not maximal load. Keep the weights honest and light.
Rotate this in every 2-3 weeks even if your shoulders feel fine — it's cheaper than fixing an injury later.
If you feel any pinching pain (not just fatigue) on any movement, stop that exercise and swap it out.

name: Arms - Biceps & Triceps Superset Blast
warmup: Light band curls and pushdowns, 1x15 each

exercises.name: Barbell Curl (superset with) / Close-Grip Bench Press
exercises.notes: Superset — no rest between the curl and the press, rest only after both are done.

exercises.name: Hammer Curl (superset with) / Rope Pushdown
exercises.notes: Same superset structure — keep the pace brisk between the pair.

exercises.name: Incline Dumbbell Curl (superset with) / Overhead Triceps Extension
exercises.notes: Full stretch on both movements at the bottom of each rep.

exercises.name: Cable Curl (superset with) / Cable Kickback
exercises.notes: Finisher pair — light weight, full burn.

cooldown: Biceps wall stretch, 30 sec each side
Overhead triceps stretch, 30 sec each side
Forearm stretch, 20 sec each side
coachTips: Supersetting opposing muscle groups (biceps/triceps) lets one recover while the other works — efficient and effective.
Keep transitions between the paired exercises under 15 seconds to get the full conditioning benefit.
Arm pump will be intense by the end — that's the intended stimulus for this session.

name: Arms - Strength-Focused Heavy Day
warmup: Empty-bar curl and close-grip press, 1x10 each

exercises.name: Barbell Curl
exercises.notes: Heaviest weight you can manage with strict form — no swinging.

exercises.name: Close-Grip Bench Press
exercises.notes: Primary triceps mass-builder for the session.

exercises.name: Weighted Dips
exercises.notes: Add weight via belt if bodyweight is manageable for 10+ reps.

exercises.name: Incline Dumbbell Curl
exercises.notes: Second heaviest biceps movement — full stretch position.

exercises.name: Skull Crushers
exercises.notes: EZ-bar preferred if available, easier on the wrists.

cooldown: Biceps wall stretch, 30 sec each side
Overhead triceps stretch, 30 sec each side
coachTips: Strength-focused arm work builds the foundation that higher-rep pump work later just adds size on top of.
Elbow health matters long term — if close-grip bench bothers your elbows, widen the grip slightly.
Progress the load slowly here, roughly 2.5kg per side every couple of weeks once reps are solid.

name: Arms - Cable-Only Constant Tension
warmup: Light cable curls and pushdowns, 1x15 each

exercises.name: Cable Curl (Straight Bar)
exercises.notes: Constant tension throughout, no dead spot like a free-weight curl has.

exercises.name: Rope Triceps Pushdown
exercises.notes: Spread the rope apart at the bottom of every rep.

exercises.name: Cable Hammer Curl (Rope)
exercises.notes: Neutral grip, targets the brachialis for arm thickness.

exercises.name: Overhead Cable Triceps Extension
exercises.notes: Full stretch behind the head, elbows fixed.

exercises.name: Single-Arm Cable Curl
exercises.notes: Corrects any left/right imbalance from the bilateral work earlier.

exercises.name: Single-Arm Cable Pushdown
exercises.notes: Same unilateral logic, applied to triceps.

cooldown: Biceps wall stretch, 30 sec each side
Overhead triceps stretch, 30 sec each side
Forearm stretch, 20 sec each side
coachTips: Cable work removes momentum from the equation entirely — every rep is honest tension.
Great low-fatigue option to slot in even on a day you're feeling generally beat up elsewhere.
The whole session needs just one cable station if your gym is busy — efficient to squeeze in.

name: Arms - Dumbbell 21s & Drop Sets

exercises.name: Dumbbell 21s Curl
exercises.notes: Classic burnout technique — expect serious biceps fatigue by the third set.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Skull Crushers (Drop Set)
exercises.notes: Have a lighter pair of dumbbells ready to grab immediately after the first 10 reps.

exercises.name: Incline Dumbbell Curl
exercises.notes: Full stretch position, controlled tempo.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Kickback
exercises.notes: Torso hinged forward, upper arm parallel to the floor throughout.

exercises.name: Zottman Curl
exercises.notes: Curl up palms-up, rotate, lower palms-down — trains biceps and forearms together.

cooldown: Biceps wall stretch, 30 sec each side
Overhead triceps stretch, 30 sec each side
coachTips: 21s and drop sets are intensity techniques — use them sparingly (once every week or two) so they stay effective.
Expect noticeable next-day soreness after this session — plan an easier session or rest day after.
Have your second set of dumbbells staged next to you before starting the drop set so you don't waste time hunting for them.

name: Arms - Forearm & Grip Emphasis

exercises.name: Barbell Curl
exercises.notes: Standard biceps builder to open the session.

exercises.name: Rope Pushdown
exercises.notes: Triceps counterpart to balance the session.

exercises.name: Reverse Barbell Curl
exercises.notes: Overhand grip, heavily loads the forearm extensors.

exercises.name: Wrist Curl
exercises.notes: Forearms resting on thighs or a bench, full range at the wrist only.

exercises.name: Reverse Wrist Curl
exercises.notes: Same setup, palms facing down — targets the opposing forearm muscles.

exercises.name: Farmer's Carry
exercises.notes: Heavy dumbbells, walk tall with shoulders back — grip will be the limiting factor, not the legs.

cooldown: Forearm stretch (flexors), 20 sec each side
Forearm stretch (extensors), 20 sec each side
Biceps wall stretch, 30 sec each side
coachTips: Grip strength is a genuinely underrated carryover to every pulling lift in this whole library — deadlifts, rows, pull-ups.
Farmer's carries are simple but brutal — start with a weight you can hold for the full 40 seconds without dropping it.
If your grip is a limiter on other sessions' pulling movements, this workout directly addresses that.

name: Core & Abs - Weighted Strength Circuit

exercises.name: Cable Crunch
exercises.notes: Kneel facing the cable, crunch from the ribs down toward the hips, not the neck.

exercises.name: Hanging Leg Raise
exercises.notes: Control the descent — swinging turns this into momentum, not core work.

exercises.name: Weighted Russian Twist
exercises.notes: Plate or dumbbell held at the chest, rotate from the torso not just the arms.

exercises.name: Ab Wheel Rollout
exercises.notes: From the knees, only go as far as you can maintain a flat lower back.

exercises.name: Weighted Plank
exercises.notes: Small plate on the upper back for added load, keep hips level.

cooldown: Cobra stretch, 30 sec
Child's pose, 45 sec
Seated spinal twist, 20 sec each side
coachTips: Weighted core work builds real strength, not just visible abs — this carries directly into your squat and deadlift bracing.
If the ab wheel is too advanced from the knees, regress to a shorter range of motion rather than skipping it.
Visible abs come from body fat percentage, not from ab exercises alone — this session builds the muscle underneath.

name: Core & Abs - Anti-Rotation & Stability

exercises.name: Pallof Press
exercises.notes: Cable at chest height, press straight out and resist the rotation pulling you sideways.

exercises.name: Suitcase Carry
exercises.notes: Single heavy dumbbell, walk tall without leaning toward the loaded side.

exercises.name: Renegade Row
exercises.notes: Anti-rotation demand from the plank position is the main point, not the row itself.

exercises.name: Side Plank
exercises.notes: Stack hips and shoulders, don't let the hips sag toward the floor.

exercises.name: Bird Dog
exercises.notes: Extend opposite arm and leg, keep the lower back flat, no rocking.

cooldown: Seated spinal twist, 20 sec each side
Child's pose, 45 sec
coachTips: Anti-rotation training is what actually protects your spine during heavy squats and deadlifts — arguably more useful than crunches.
These movements look easy — they're not. Resist the urge to add load before your form is airtight.
Great low-impact session to slot in on a day your joints need a break from heavier lifting.

name: Core & Abs - Bodyweight Burnout
warmup: 3 min jump rope or brisk walk

exercises.name: Bicycle Crunch
exercises.notes: Slow and controlled beats fast and sloppy — full rotation each rep.

exercises.name: Mountain Climbers
exercises.notes: Keep hips low and level, don't let them pike up.

exercises.name: Plank
exercises.notes: Straight line from shoulders to ankles, squeeze the glutes.

exercises.name: Leg Raises
exercises.notes: Lower back pressed into the floor the whole time, don't let it arch.

exercises.name: Flutter Kicks
exercises.notes: Small, quick kicks, lower back stays glued to the floor.

exercises.name: V-Ups
exercises.notes: Finisher — reach hands to feet at the top of each rep.

cooldown: Cobra stretch, 30 sec
Child's pose, 45 sec
Seated spinal twist, 20 sec each side
coachTips: No equipment needed — good session for a day the gym floor is packed or you're pressed for time.
This is a metabolic burnout session as much as a strength one — expect your heart rate to climb.
Quality over speed on every movement — a controlled bicycle crunch beats a fast sloppy one.

name: Core & Abs - Lower Back & Spinal Health

exercises.name: Back Extension (45-Degree Bench)
exercises.notes: Hinge from the hips, squeeze glutes at the top — don't hyperextend the lower back.

exercises.name: Dead Bug
exercises.notes: Lower back stays pressed into the floor the entire set.

exercises.name: Bird Dog
exercises.notes: Slow and controlled, no rocking side to side.

exercises.name: Glute Bridge
exercises.notes: Squeeze glutes hard at the top, avoid over-arching the lower back.

exercises.name: Superman Hold
exercises.notes: Lift chest and legs slightly, hold — a gentle way to strengthen the erectors.

exercises.name: Plank
exercises.notes: Finisher, straight line, no sagging hips.

cooldown: Child's pose, 60 sec
Knee-to-chest stretch, 30 sec each side
Cat-cow, 8 reps
coachTips: This session is specifically designed to build a resilient lower back to support your heavier squat and deadlift days.
None of these movements should cause pain — mild fatigue is expected, sharp pain is not. Stop if you feel the latter.
A strong, well-conditioned lower back is one of the best injury-prevention investments you can make.

name: Core & Abs - Rotational Power & Obliques

exercises.name: Cable Woodchopper (High to Low)
exercises.notes: Rotate from the torso and hips together, pivot the back foot.

exercises.name: Russian Twist (Weighted)
exercises.notes: Feet can stay down for more control if balance is limiting the load.

exercises.name: Kettlebell Windmill
exercises.notes: Light kettlebell locked overhead, hinge at the hip while keeping eyes on the bell.

exercises.name: Side Plank with Rotation
exercises.notes: Thread the free arm under the torso then rotate it back up to the ceiling.

exercises.name: Standing Oblique Crunch (Cable)
exercises.notes: High pulley, crunch the elbow toward the same-side hip.

cooldown: Seated spinal twist, 20 sec each side
Child's pose, 45 sec
coachTips: Rotational core strength carries over to almost every real-world movement — twisting, throwing, changing direction.
Control the rotation on woodchoppers — this isn't about how fast you can whip the cable across.
Start light on kettlebell windmills — the shoulder position is unfamiliar for most lifters at first.

name: Full Body - Compound Strength Circuit

exercises.name: Barbell Back Squat
exercises.notes: Sets the tone for the session — biggest lift first while fresh.

exercises.name: Barbell Bench Press
exercises.notes: Main upper-body pushing movement.

exercises.name: Barbell Bent-Over Row
exercises.notes: Balances the pressing volume with pulling volume.

exercises.name: Standing Overhead Press
exercises.notes: Full-body tension needed to stabilize the bar overhead.

exercises.name: Romanian Deadlift
exercises.notes: Posterior chain finisher on the lower body.

exercises.name: Plank
exercises.notes: Core finisher to close out the session.

cooldown: Standing quad stretch, 30 sec each side
Doorway chest stretch, 30 sec each side
Lat stretch on rack, 30 sec each side
coachTips: This is the highest total-body demand session in the library — make sure you're well fed and rested beforehand.
The big five lifts here (squat, bench, row, press, RDL) cover almost every major muscle group in one session.
If total volume feels like too much on a given day, trim the accessory sets before trimming the compound lifts.

name: Full Body - Dumbbell Only Session

exercises.name: Dumbbell Goblet Squat
exercises.notes: Heaviest dumbbell you can hold at the chest with good depth.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Bench Press
exercises.notes: Full range of motion, control the descent.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Bent-Over Row
exercises.notes: Flat back, pull to the lower ribs.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Romanian Deadlift
exercises.notes: Soft knees, hinge at the hips, feel the hamstring stretch.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Shoulder Press
exercises.notes: Seated or standing, either works.

exercises.name: Renegade Row
exercises.notes: Core and back finisher combined into one movement.

cooldown: Standing quad stretch, 30 sec each side
Doorway chest stretch, 30 sec each side
Lat stretch on rack, 30 sec each side
coachTips: This session works even on a busy gym day when the squat rack and bench are both taken — a dumbbell station is enough.
Great option for travel gyms with limited equipment too.
Keep dumbbell weight selection honest — the last 2-3 reps of every set should feel genuinely hard.

name: Full Body - Functional Circuit

exercises.name: Kettlebell Swing
exercises.notes: Hip-hinge power movement, not a squat — the kettlebell floats from hip drive.

exercises.name: Goblet Squat to Press
exercises.notes: Combine a squat and an overhead press into one fluid movement.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Thruster
exercises.notes: Front squat into a press — full body, high heart rate.

exercises.name: Renegade Row to Push-Up
exercises.notes: Combines pulling, anti-rotation, and pressing in one circuit round.

exercises.name: Kettlebell Goblet Reverse Lunge
exercises.notes: Finisher — controlled pace even as fatigue sets in.

cooldown: Standing quad stretch, 30 sec each side
Child's pose, 45 sec
Doorway chest stretch, 30 sec each side
coachTips: This session blends strength and conditioning — expect your heart rate to stay elevated throughout, not just during the cardio block.
Combination movements (squat-to-press, thrusters) demand more coordination — start lighter than your isolated lift numbers.
Great option when time is tight but you still want full-body coverage.

name: Full Body - Machine Circuit (Joint-Friendly)

exercises.name: Leg Press
exercises.notes: Lower-impact way to load the legs heavily.

exercises.name: Machine Chest Press
exercises.notes: Fixed path takes stabilizer demand out of the equation.

exercises.name: Seated Row Machine
exercises.notes: Pull with the elbows, squeeze the shoulder blades together.

exercises.name: Machine Shoulder Press
exercises.notes: Joint-friendly overhead pressing option.

exercises.name: Leg Curl (Machine)
exercises.notes: Hamstring finisher for the lower body.

exercises.name: Cable Crunch
exercises.notes: Core finisher to close the session.

cooldown: Standing quad stretch, 30 sec each side
Doorway chest stretch, 30 sec each side
Lat stretch on rack, 30 sec each side
coachTips: A good full-body option on weeks your joints need a break from barbell loading while still training hard.
Machines let you push each set closer to true failure safely — use that advantage.
This session pairs well as a lighter bookend the week after a heavy squat/bench/deadlift full-body day.

name: Full Body - Strength Endurance

exercises.name: Barbell Squat
exercises.notes: Higher reps, moderate weight — this session trains strength endurance, not max strength.

exercises.name: Push-Ups
exercises.notes: Full range every rep, even as fatigue accumulates.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Row
exercises.notes: Keep the pace brisk between sides.

exercises.name: Walking Lunges
exercises.notes: Continuous walking pattern, don't rest mid-set.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Shoulder Press
exercises.notes: Moderate weight, focus on maintaining pace over the set.

exercises.name: Plank
exercises.notes: Core finisher.

cooldown: Standing quad stretch, 30 sec each side
Child's pose, 45 sec
coachTips: Shorter rest periods here on purpose — this session blends strength work with a genuine conditioning effect.
Expect your heart rate to stay elevated between sets, unlike the heavier strength-focused sessions in this library.
A great fat-loss-oriented session when time is limited but intensity needs to stay high.

name: Cardio & Conditioning - Steady-State Fat Loss
warmup: 3 min flat treadmill walk to ease in

exercises.name: Incline Treadmill Walk
exercises.notes: 6-8% incline, pace where you can talk in short sentences but not hold a full conversation.

cooldown: 3 min flat walk to bring heart rate down
Standing quad stretch, 30 sec each side
Calf stretch on wall, 30 sec each side
coachTips: Zone 2 steady-state is one of the most sustainable fat-loss tools available — it's genuinely low-fatigue relative to the calories burned.
This is a good session to pair with a rest day from lifting, or right after an upper-body session.
Track incline and speed weekly — small progressive increases here are real fitness gains.

name: Cardio & Conditioning - Rowing Intervals

exercises.name: Rowing Intervals
exercises.notes: Push a hard, consistent pace on each 500m — aim for a similar split time on every interval.

cardio.intensity: High during work, Low during rest

cooldown: 5 min easy-pace rowing
Seated hamstring stretch, 30 sec each side
Lat stretch on rack, 30 sec each side
coachTips: Focus on rowing technique before speed — legs drive first, then hips open, then arms pull.
If your split time drops noticeably after interval 5-6, you started too fast — pace yourself better next session.
Rowing is one of the few cardio modalities that also meaningfully trains the back — a nice bonus for a fat-loss-focused conditioning day.

name: Cardio & Conditioning - Bike Endurance Ride

exercises.name: Stationary Bike Ride
exercises.notes: Moderate resistance, cadence around 80-90 RPM, consistent effort throughout.

cooldown: 5 min easy pedaling to cool down
Standing quad stretch, 30 sec each side
Seated hamstring stretch, 30 sec each side
coachTips: Cycling is one of the most joint-friendly cardio options available — a great choice if knees are feeling beat up from leg day.
Keep cadence steady rather than mashing hard at low RPM — smoother pedaling is easier on the knees long term.
This is a good low-fatigue conditioning session to schedule the day after a heavy leg workout.

name: Cardio & Conditioning - StairMaster Climb

exercises.name: StairMaster Steady Climb
exercises.notes: Full foot on each step, upright posture — don't lean on the handrails to take load off your legs.

cooldown: 3 min slow-pace stepping to cool down
Standing quad stretch, 30 sec each side
Calf stretch on wall, 30 sec each side
coachTips: The StairMaster is deceptively tough — 25 minutes here burns comparably to a much longer treadmill session.
Letting go of the handrails (even lightly resting fingertips only) roughly doubles the leg demand — build up to it.
Great glute-and-leg-friendly conditioning option that doubles as light active recovery for the upper body.

name: Cardio & Conditioning - Mixed Modality Endurance

exercises.name: Treadmill Block
exercises.notes: Incline walk or light jog, moderate steady effort.

exercises.name: Rowing Block
exercises.notes: Smooth, consistent stroke rate, moderate effort.

exercises.name: Bike Block
exercises.notes: Moderate resistance, steady cadence.

cooldown: 3 min easy walk
Full-body light stretching, 5 min
coachTips: Rotating machines keeps the same muscles from getting overly fatigued in one pattern while total cardio volume stays high.
This is a nice mental-variety session — 40 minutes on one machine can drag, 3 blocks of 12 rarely does.
Good option when you want a solid conditioning session without excessive impact on any single joint.

name: HIIT - Bodyweight Tabata Circuit
warmup: 3 min jumping jacks and dynamic stretching

exercises.name: Tabata Circuit: Squat Jumps / Push-Ups / Mountain Climbers / Burpees
exercises.reps: 20 sec work / 10 sec rest, rotating through the 4 movements
exercises.notes: Classic Tabata timing — 8 total rounds of 20 sec on, 10 sec off, cycling through all 4 exercises.

cooldown: 3 min slow walk to bring heart rate down
Standing quad stretch, 30 sec each side
Child's pose, 45 sec
coachTips: True Tabata means genuinely maximal effort on the 20-second work windows — this should feel very hard by round 5-6.
At 108kg, burpees and squat jumps carry real impact load — land soft, and regress to step-back burpees if knees complain.
This is a short but brutal session — 16 minutes of actual work can rival 45 minutes of steady cardio in effect.

name: HIIT - Assault Bike Sprints

exercises.name: Assault Bike Sprint Intervals
exercises.notes: 10 rounds of 20 sec max effort, 40 sec easy recovery pace — don't fully stop during the rest.

cooldown: 3 min easy pedaling
Standing quad stretch, 30 sec each side
Seated hamstring stretch, 30 sec each side
coachTips: The assault bike is unforgiving — pace the first 2-3 sprints slightly conservatively so you can hold effort through round 10.
This one hits both upper and lower body simultaneously, unlike a treadmill sprint — expect arms to fatigue too.
One of the most time-efficient conditioning sessions in this whole library — 10 minutes of real work.

name: HIIT - Dumbbell Complex Circuit

exercises.name: Dumbbell Complex: Deadlift + Row + Clean + Press + Squat
exercises.reps: 6 reps of each movement back-to-back, no rest between exercises
exercises.notes: Same pair of dumbbells the whole complex — don't set them down until all 5 movements are done.

cooldown: 3 min easy walk
Doorway chest stretch, 30 sec each side
Standing quad stretch, 30 sec each side
coachTips: Complexes build serious conditioning because the dumbbells never touch the floor — grip and heart rate both take a beating.
Choose a weight you could comfortably clean and press for 10+ reps on its own — the complex will make it feel much heavier by round 4.
Technique breaks down under fatigue faster in complexes than in isolated lifts — slow down rather than let form slip.

name: HIIT - Kettlebell Swing Ladder

exercises.name: Kettlebell Swing Ladder (10-20-30-20-10)
exercises.notes: Total of 90 swings across the ladder — power from the hips, not the arms or lower back.

cooldown: 3 min easy walk
Figure-4 glute stretch, 30 sec each side
Seated hamstring stretch, 30 sec each side
coachTips: The swing is a hip-hinge power movement — the kettlebell should feel like it's floating up from hip drive, not being lifted by the arms.
By the 30-rep step your grip and lower back will both be tested — that's the point of the ladder format.
If lower back rounds under fatigue on later sets, drop the kettlebell weight rather than push through bad form.

name: HIIT - Sled Push & Sprint Intervals

exercises.name: Sled Push (moderate load) + 20m Sprint (bodyweight or light bike sprint if no open floor)
exercises.notes: If a sprint lane isn't available, substitute a 20-second all-out effort on the assault bike after each sled push.

cooldown: 3 min easy walk
Standing quad stretch, 30 sec each side
Calf stretch on wall, 30 sec each side
coachTips: This is one of the most demanding sessions in the library — genuinely elite-level conditioning work. Respect the recovery afterward.
Sled pushes are extremely joint-friendly for the load they provide since there's no eccentric/lowering phase — knee-safe even at heavier loads.
If this leaves you unusually sore, swap it for a lighter HIIT variation next time and revisit once conditioning improves.

name: Mobility & Recovery - Full Body Flow
warmup: 3 min easy walk to raise core temperature

exercises.name: Foam Roll: Quads, Hamstrings, Lats, Upper Back
exercises.notes: Slow, controlled rolling — pause on tender spots for 10-15 seconds rather than rolling faster.

exercises.name: World's Greatest Stretch
exercises.notes: Full-body mobility flow hitting hips, hamstrings, and thoracic spine in one movement.

exercises.name: Cat-Cow
exercises.notes: Sync the movement with your breath, full spinal range.

exercises.name: 90/90 Hip Switch
exercises.notes: Great for hip internal/external rotation, often neglected in lifting programs.

exercises.name: Thread the Needle
exercises.notes: Thoracic spine rotation — helpful for shoulder and upper back mobility.

cooldown: Seated forward fold, 60 sec
Deep breathing, 5 slow breaths
coachTips: This session is a genuine training day, not a day off — mobility work directly improves your squat depth and overhead press range.
Nothing here should ever feel painful — mild tension and stretch is the target sensation.
Schedule this the day after your heaviest lifting session of the week to aid recovery.

name: Mobility & Recovery - Hip & Ankle Focus

exercises.name: Foam Roll: Calves, Quads, IT Band
exercises.notes: IT band rolling is uncomfortable for most people — go slow and reduce pressure if too intense.

exercises.name: Ankle Dorsiflexion Rock
exercises.notes: Kneeling position, rock knee over toes without heel lifting — directly improves squat depth.

exercises.name: Deep Squat Hold
exercises.notes: Hold onto a rack or post for support if balance is limiting at first.

exercises.name: Pigeon Pose
exercises.notes: One of the best hip-opener stretches available — breathe deeply throughout.

exercises.name: 90/90 Hip Switch
exercises.notes: Continues the hip mobility theme from earlier in the session.

cooldown: Standing quad stretch, 30 sec each side
Deep breathing, 5 slow breaths
coachTips: Limited ankle mobility is one of the most common (and fixable) causes of poor squat depth — this session directly targets that.
Hip mobility work pays off on deadlift setup and lunge/split squat stability too.
Consistency matters more than intensity here — 20 minutes twice a week beats one long session once a month.

name: Mobility & Recovery - Upper Body & Thoracic Spine

exercises.name: Foam Roll: Upper Back, Lats
exercises.notes: Support your head with your hands while rolling the upper back.

exercises.name: Thread the Needle
exercises.notes: Thoracic rotation — most desk-bound lifters are noticeably restricted here.

exercises.name: Band Shoulder Dislocates
exercises.notes: Wide grip on the band, move slowly through the full overhead range.

exercises.name: Scapular Wall Slides
exercises.notes: Maintain contact with the wall throughout the whole movement.

exercises.name: Doorway Chest Stretch
exercises.notes: Great counter to hours spent in a pressing-dominant training pattern.

cooldown: Child's pose, 60 sec
Deep breathing, 5 slow breaths
coachTips: Upper body mobility directly improves overhead press range and bench press bar path — this isn't just 'flexibility for its own sake.'
Band shoulder dislocates should feel like a stretch, never a pinch — widen your grip if you feel pinching.
A great session to pair with a desk-heavy work day to counter hours of forward-rounded posture.

name: Mobility & Recovery - Yoga-Inspired Flow

exercises.name: Sun Salutation Flow
exercises.notes: Down dog, plank, cobra, forward fold, repeat — move with the breath, not against a clock.

exercises.name: Warrior II Hold
exercises.notes: Builds hip and leg mobility along with a bit of strength endurance.

exercises.name: Pigeon Pose
exercises.notes: Deep hip opener, breathe into any tightness rather than forcing the stretch.

exercises.name: Seated Forward Fold
exercises.notes: Hamstring and lower back release.

exercises.name: Supine Spinal Twist
exercises.notes: Gentle spinal decompression to finish.

cooldown: Savasana (lying still), 3 min
Deep breathing, 5 slow breaths
coachTips: This is as much about downregulating the nervous system as it is about flexibility — a good session for a genuinely tired week.
No need for prior yoga experience — move at your own pace and skip anything that feels sharp rather than stretchy.
Pairs well the day before a heavy squat or deadlift session to arrive fresher.

name: Mobility & Recovery - Lower Body Deep Stretch

exercises.name: Foam Roll: Hamstrings, Quads, Glutes, Calves
exercises.notes: Full lower-body rolling sweep — this session is dedicated entirely to leg recovery.

exercises.name: Standing Quad Stretch
exercises.notes: Hold onto something for balance, keep the knee pointed straight down.

exercises.name: Seated Hamstring Stretch
exercises.notes: Flat back, hinge from the hips rather than rounding the spine.

exercises.name: Figure-4 Glute Stretch
exercises.notes: Lying on the floor or a bench, whichever is more comfortable.

exercises.name: Calf Stretch on Wall
exercises.notes: Bent-knee variation afterward targets the soleus specifically.

cooldown: Legs-up-the-wall, 3 min
Deep breathing, 5 slow breaths
coachTips: Schedule this the day after your heaviest leg session — it directly speeds up recovery from squat and deadlift soreness.
Hold each stretch to a point of mild tension, never pain — breathe slowly and let the muscle release over time.
Legs-up-the-wall at the end is a simple, effective way to help with lower-leg swelling and circulation after heavy training.

name: Active Recovery - Easy Walk & Stretch
warmup: 2 min slow walk to ease in

exercises.name: Flat Treadmill Walk
exercises.notes: You should be able to hold a full conversation comfortably the entire time.

exercises.name: Full-Body Static Stretch Sequence
exercises.notes: Quads, hamstrings, chest, lats, shoulders — 45 sec each, whatever feels tightest that day.

cooldown: Deep breathing, 5 slow breaths
coachTips: The whole point of active recovery is that it should feel easy — if you're breathing hard, slow down.
Light movement genuinely speeds up recovery better than doing nothing at all, by promoting blood flow to fatigued muscles.
This is an ideal session the day after your toughest workout of the week, or on a day you're feeling run down.

name: Active Recovery - Easy Bike Spin

exercises.name: Easy Stationary Bike Spin
exercises.notes: Low resistance, high cadence — this should feel almost too easy, and that's correct.

cooldown: Standing quad stretch, 30 sec each side
Seated hamstring stretch, 30 sec each side
Deep breathing, 5 slow breaths
coachTips: Cycling at an easy spin is one of the gentlest ways to flush blood through fatigued legs after a heavy squat or deadlift day.
Resist the temptation to turn this into a real workout — the value here is specifically in the low intensity.
If you feel great and want to push harder, that's a sign to schedule a real Cardio & Conditioning session instead of pushing this one further.

name: Active Recovery - Pool Walk / Swim (Light)
warmup: 2 min easy entry, gentle movement in the water

exercises.name: Pool Walking / Easy Swimming
exercises.notes: Water buoyancy takes significant load off the joints — great option if the gym floor has a pool available.

cooldown: Gentle full-body stretching out of the water, 5 min
Deep breathing, 5 slow breaths
coachTips: Water-based recovery is especially useful at higher bodyweight since it dramatically reduces joint compression.
This is a genuinely restorative session — don't turn it into a lap-swimming workout.
If a pool isn't available, swap this for the Easy Walk & Stretch or Easy Bike Spin variation instead.

name: Active Recovery - Light Mobility Walk

exercises.name: Flat Treadmill Walk
exercises.notes: Relaxed pace throughout.

exercises.name: World's Greatest Stretch
exercises.notes: Combines walking recovery with light mobility work.

exercises.name: Cat-Cow
exercises.notes: Gentle spinal movement to finish.

exercises.name: Deep Squat Hold
exercises.notes: Very light — just a gentle range-of-motion check-in, not a strength exercise today.

cooldown: Deep breathing, 5 slow breaths
coachTips: This blends the two lightest categories in the library — walking and mobility — into one easy session.
A good choice specifically the day after a HIIT or sled push session when the whole body needs a break.
If you feel genuinely fatigued rather than just muscle-sore, consider a full rest day instead of even this session.

name: Active Recovery - Elliptical Easy Session

exercises.name: Elliptical Easy Session
exercises.notes: Zero-impact on the joints — a good option when even walking feels like too much that day.

cooldown: Full-body light stretching, 5 min
Deep breathing, 5 slow breaths
coachTips: The elliptical's zero-impact nature makes it one of the gentlest cardio options in the whole library.
This should feel refreshing, not depleting — if it doesn't, drop the resistance further.
A solid rotation choice for active recovery days specifically when joints feel achy rather than muscles feeling sore.
